title: OpenAI open-sources MRC, a networking protocol for 131,000-GPU clusters
subtitle: Built with AMD, Broadcom, Intel, Microsoft and Nvidia, MRC compresses three-tier Ethernet fabrics to two and recovers from link failures in microseconds.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI, alongside AMD, Broadcom, Intel, Microsoft and Nvidia, contributed a new networking protocol called Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) to the Open Compute Project. MRC lets a single GPU-to-GPU transfer spread across hundreds of network paths, route around failures in microseconds, and connect roughly 131,000 GPUs using only two Ethernet switch tiers instead of the three or four tiers prior 800Gb/s designs required.

The why is brutally practical. Training a frontier model is bottlenecked at least as much by the network as by the GPUs themselves: a single hung link can stall an entire run, and rebuilding state from a checkpoint costs hours of wall-clock time on a cluster that burns millions of dollars a day. Cutting a switch tier removes latency and cost; routing around failures in microseconds means trainings stop noticing them at all.

MRC is already deployed inside OpenAI's largest Nvidia GB200 supercomputers, including the Abilene, Texas site operated with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure and Microsoft's Fairwater facilities. By contributing the spec to OCP instead of keeping it proprietary, OpenAI has set a default that AMD's Pensando NICs and Nvidia's Spectrum-X both support — meaning the rest of the industry will adopt it whether or not they trained with OpenAI.

For learners: the AI capability story usually focuses on the model. The economic story is increasingly about plumbing — networking, power, cooling, and checkpointing. If you are curious where the real engineering bottlenecks sit, read OCP contributions like this one rather than press releases. The constraints that show up here are the ones that will shape what models can be trained next.
